name: Rob Sumner
bio: Rob Sumner broke into junior hockey with the 1986-87 Richmond Sockeyes, posting 19 points and 103 penalty minutes in 44 games as the team captured the Centennial Cup. He moved on to a three-season stint with the WHL's Victoria Cougars, followed by a brief stop with the Prince Albert Raiders. Sumner later turned professional in the ECHL, playing for the Louisville Icehawks and South Carolina Stingrays between 1992 and 1995.

name: Mike Claringbull
bio: Mike Claringbull was born November 29, 1966 in Pincher Creek, Alberta. He played junior A in the Alberta Junior Hockey League with the Hobbema Hawks before two WHL seasons with the Medicine Hat Tigers, where he was drafted by the Montreal Canadiens in 1985. He joined the Richmond Sockeyes for the 1986-87 championship run, contributing on the blue line through the playoff drive.

name: Russ Goglin
bio: Russ Goglin's junior hockey career ran from 1983 to 1987, split between the BCJHL (Merritt Centennials/Warriors, Richmond Sockeyes) and brief WHL stops with Kamloops, Seattle, and Victoria. He joined the Richmond Sockeyes during the 1985-86 season and returned for part of their 1986-87 Centennial Cup-winning campaign.

name: Mike McCormick
bio: Mike McCormick was one of the Richmond Sockeyes' leading offensive contributors in 1986-87, producing 50 points and 129 PIM in 51 games en route to the Centennial Cup. He then moved to NCAA Division I hockey at the University of North Dakota (WCHA) from 1987 to 1990. McCormick turned professional with the ECHL's Greensboro Monarchs in 1989, playing more than a decade in the ECHL, CHL, and WPHL with stops including the Johnstown Chiefs, Fort Worth Fire, Wichita Thunder, Amarillo Rattlers, and Fort Worth Brahmas through the late 1990s.

name: Stan Czenczek
bio: Stan Czenczek began his junior career with the Nanaimo Clippers of the BCJHL in 1983-84 before attending the University of Maine (Hockey East) in 1984-85. After brief stops with the Abbotsford Falcons and Delta Flyers, he joined the Richmond Sockeyes in 1986-87, producing 36 points from the blue line in the Centennial Cup season.

name: Paul Rutherford
bio: Paul Rutherford broke in with the BCJHL's Delta Flyers in 1985-86 and joined the Richmond Sockeyes for the 1986-87 Centennial Cup season, posting 48 points in 47 games. He then spent four seasons at Ohio State University in the CCHA from 1987 to 1991, finishing as a consistent offensive contributor. He turned professional with the ECHL's Richmond Renegades in 1991-92, where he produced 58 points in 64 games.

name: Jason Phillips
bio: Jason Phillips was one of the most decorated players on the 1987 Centennial Cup champion Richmond Sockeyes. Named the tournament's Top Scorer and Most Sportsmanlike Player and selected to the All-Star team as a forward, Phillips posted 43 points in 34 games during the Sockeyes' championship run after bouncing between the BCJHL Vernon Lakers and WHL organizations in previous seasons. He returned to the WHL in 1987-88 with the Victoria Cougars and recorded 102 points in 62 games.

name: Steve Jaques
bio: Steve Jaques was a physical forward whose junior career began with the 1986-87 Centennial Cup-winning Richmond Sockeyes. He moved to the WHL with the New Westminster Bruins in 1987-88, posting 336 PIM, and continued with the Tri-City Americans for two more seasons, totaling 84 points in 1989-90. Jaques turned professional with the IHL's Phoenix Roadrunners in 1990 and played nearly a decade in the minors, including stints with the Providence Bruins (AHL), Las Vegas Thunder, Houston Aeros, and Kansas City Blades (IHL), retiring in 1998.

name: Dave Tomlinson
bio:
Dave Tomlinson was born May 8, 1969 in North Vancouver, British Columbia. After a 48-goal season with the Summerland Buckaroos, he joined the Richmond Sockeyes for 1986-87 and captained the team to the Centennial Cup with 108 points in 51 regular-season games. 

 He accepted a scholarship to Boston University, where he played four seasons in Hockey East (1987-1991) and recorded 179 career college points. Drafted by the Toronto Maple Leafs in the 1989 NHL Supplemental Draft, Tomlinson played 42 NHL games over his career with Toronto, Winnipeg, and Florida, along with significant time in the AHL (St. John's, Moncton) and IHL (Cincinnati Cyclones). 

 He spent roughly a decade playing professionally in Germany's DEL — primarily with Adler Mannheim — before retiring in 2006. Post-playing, Tomlinson has served as a color commentator for Vancouver Canucks radio broadcasts and was named the inaugural radio analyst for the Seattle Kraken in 2021.

name: Bryon Moller
bio: Bryon Moller was a forward on the 1986-87 Richmond Sockeyes, contributing 25 points in 16 games during the Centennial Cup-winning season.

name: Brian Kozak
bio: Brian Kozak (born 1966) began his junior career with the OHL's Soo Greyhounds and OJHL's Newmarket Flyers in 1983-84. He joined the Richmond Sockeyes for 1986-87 after a brief WHL stint with New Westminster, posting 49 points in 39 games during the Centennial Cup run. Kozak then played a season at the University of Manitoba (CWUAA) before moving into coaching; he served as head coach of the BCHL's Quesnel Millionaires in 2005-06.

name: Bill Hardy
bio: Bill Hardy played four seasons with the Nanaimo Clippers of the BCJHL beginning in 1982-83, highlighted by a 72-point 1985-86 season. He moved to the Richmond Sockeyes for 1986-87, contributing 52 points during the Centennial Cup title run. He later played college hockey at Red Deer College (ACAC) in 1988-89.

name: Rob Clarke
bio: Rob Clarke was a forward on the 1986-87 Richmond Sockeyes, leading the team in goal scoring during the Centennial Cup-winning season with 43 goals and 66 points in 33 games.

name: Tony Bobbitt
bio: Tony Bobbitt (born May 22, 1968) played junior hockey from 1984 to 1991. After stops with the Salmon Arm Totems, Shuswap Blazers, and New Westminster Bruins, he joined the Richmond Sockeyes for the 1986-87 Centennial Cup-winning season, recording 57 points and 175 PIM in 46 games. He returned for another strong BCJHL season with Richmond in 1987-88 and later played briefly with the ECHL's Johnstown Chiefs in 1990-91.

name: Jim Gunn
bio: Jim Gunn began his junior career in 1983-84 with a brief BCJHL stop before joining the WHL's Victoria Cougars. He later played for the Lethbridge Broncos (WHL) and joined the Richmond Sockeyes in 1985-86. During the 1986-87 Centennial Cup-winning season he led the team with 363 penalty minutes while adding 22 points in 46 games. He later played college hockey at Mount Royal College (ACAC) in 1988-89.

name: Dean Rutledge
bio: Dean Rutledge was born March 4, 1966 in Carmangay, Alberta. His junior career began with the SJHL's Swift Current Broncos and BCJHL's Langley Eagles in 1983-84. He played AJHL hockey with the Hobbema Hawks and Fort McMurray Oil Barons (48 points in 1985-86) before joining the Richmond Sockeyes for the 1986-87 Centennial Cup run, posting 28 points in 16 games. He later played at the Southern Alberta Institute of Technology (ACAC).

name: Trevor Dickie
bio: Trevor Dickie played AJHL junior hockey with the Olds Grizzlys in 1983-84 before moving to the WHL's New Westminster Bruins. After a year at the University of Calgary (CWUAA), he joined the Richmond Sockeyes for 1986-87, piling up 259 PIM in 41 games during the Centennial Cup run. He returned to the University of Calgary for multiple seasons through 1991-92.

name: Matt Hervey
bio: Matt Hervey was born May 16, 1966 in Whittier, California. After junior time with the Langley Eagles (BCJHL) and three WHL seasons split between Victoria and Lethbridge, he joined the Richmond Sockeyes mid-season in 1986-87 and was named to the Centennial Cup All-Star Team as a defenceman during the championship run. \n\n He turned professional in 1987-88 with the AHL's Moncton Hawks, where he played five seasons and established himself as a rugged blueliner. Hervey made his NHL debut with the Winnipeg Jets in 1988-89 and went on to appear in 35 NHL games over his career with the Jets, Boston Bruins, and Tampa Bay Lightning. He finished his playing days in the IHL with Atlanta, Milwaukee, and Los Angeles, retiring in 1996.

name: Jason Talo
bio: Jason Talo began his junior career with the 1986-87 Richmond Sockeyes, contributing 19 points in 39 games during the Centennial Cup-winning season. He remained with the Sockeyes for three more BCJHL seasons, peaking at 67 points in 1987-88, before finishing his junior career with the Vernon Lakers in 1989-90.

name: Frank Romeo
bio: Frank Romeo was the goaltending hero of the Richmond Sockeyes' 1987 Centennial Cup run and was named Most Valuable Player of the national championship tournament. After stops with the Revelstoke Rockets/Rangers, Vernon Lakers, and Kelowna Packers in the BCJHL, Romeo joined the Sockeyes for 1986-87, posting a 14-3 record with a 2.95 GAA and .899 save percentage in 21 appearances during the championship year. He later played for Brandon University in the CWUAA.

name: Jamie Stewart
bio: Jamie Stewart was a goaltender on the 1986-87 Richmond Sockeyes, part of the Centennial Cup championship roster.

name: Chris Dickson
bio: Chris Dickson was a goaltender on the 1986-87 Richmond Sockeyes, part of the Centennial Cup championship roster.

name: Orland Kurtenbach
bio:
Orland John Kurtenbach was born September 7, 1936 in Cudworth, Saskatchewan. A long-time NHL centreman known for his defensive play and toughness, he played twenty professional seasons for the Boston Bruins, New York Rangers, Toronto Maple Leafs, and Vancouver Canucks, becoming the Canucks' inaugural captain following their 1970 NHL expansion. Over 639 NHL games he recorded 119 goals and 213 assists for 332 points with 628 penalty minutes, and scored the first hat trick in Canucks history on December 12, 1970. 

 After retiring as a player in 1974, Kurtenbach moved into coaching with stints for the Seattle Totems (1974-75), Tulsa Oilers (1975-77), the Vancouver Canucks as NHL head coach (1976-78, 36-62-27 record), and the AHL's Springfield Indians (1982-83). He took the bench of the BCJHL's Richmond Sockeyes in 1986 and guided the team to the 1987 Centennial Cup national Junior A championship. 

 Kurtenbach won the WHL Rookie of the Year award in 1958, the Cyclone Taylor Trophy as Canucks MVP in 1971, 1972 and 1973, and the AHL's Jake Milford Trophy in 1976. He was the first Vancouver Canucks player inducted into the franchise's Ring of Honour on October 26, 2010, and has also been recognized by the Saskatchewan Hockey Hall of Fame, the BC Hockey Hall of Fame, and the Prince Albert Sports Hall of Fame.

other.label: Prince Albert Sports Hall of Fame
